speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 3 with Sam Simchovitch. We're now talking about his escape from Russia and his journey back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, now, this was a question. They made an agreement, the Polish government, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or the Polish authorities in Russia, they made an agreement to let all the Polish citizens from Poland proper, not from Ukraine but from Poland, to return to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Jews were included. They formed in 1946. They began-- some people went on their own, but they, they organized trains where, again-- freight trains to take the people back to Poland. I hesitated. I thought maybe to stay in Russia, or do I have to go back? But everybody was going back. Only a few here and there. Some people, not in the bigger cities, or maybe they married Russian girls and so on. Most all in our area, everybody except one stayed. So we [INAUDIBLE] were taken again with I, my wife, my daughter, and my wife's sister, my sister-in-law, went into that train. Again a freight train with, with coal, with planks. With a girl, our little girl about a year old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we traveled another month to Poland, slowly back to Poland. And they send us in the new areas in Silesia, a, a small town, very popular, a lot of Jews. And then I was at [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- I became active in the Bund again, in the youth organization of Bund. They had means. They had-- they were helped from America. There were dollars, and the dollar was very high. In Poland it was 1,000 zloty to the dollar, so they had means to help. They created cooperatives and youth groups and clubs and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there for two years, and we moved from [NON-ENGLISH], the largest city, to Breslau. Wroclaw, it's called. They changed the names, the German names for the Polish name, Wroclaw, and there was a community. They were holding parties and, and so on. People were leaving for Israel as well. But then in 1948, they started to liquidate the freedom of Poland. They wanted-- they forced all the parties to join the Polish Socialist Party and the Bund and others, the Farmers' Party, to join together and form one Communist Party. And so a lot of people ran away over this, and they [INAUDIBLE] the border in Germany, to Germany. I was with a child, and I hesitated. But I applied for a visa to France. I got, I got some help to get a visa to France. And, and we, we went to France. I was eight months in France. And my wife had a brother in New York. He left Poland in the '30s, an older brother. He just died lately. And he arranged for visas to Canada. And after eight months in France, we came, we arrived 1949, in July. We arrived in Montreal. I chose-- my sponsor was Toronto, but I choose Montreal because I knew there is a Jewish Public Library, there is a, a writers' union, a newspaper, so I choose Montreal. It was hard. After a week, I went to a factory. I worked in a factory as an operator of linings of handbags because I knew how to sew. I wasn't satisfied. I didn't make much. We were living in a home with a, with another woman. And now we didn't have-- we couldn't afford our own apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, after two years in the factory, I got a chance to leave the factory to go study for a teacher, Hebrew-- I knew Yiddish well, but I didn't know Hebrew. I knew it to read in Seder. So I got a, a scholarship from Canadian Jewish Congress, about $20 a week, and we lived at somebody we rent-- in a rented room. And I began to study.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years, I study from the beginning Bible in Hebrew and also Yiddish literature and so on. Summers, I worked in libraries here and there, all kinds of-- in the factory again. And I graduated and became a Hebrew teacher. I worked for a year in, in Montreal in the [INAUDIBLE], and then I moved to Ottawa. For six years, I worked in Ottawa at the Hebrew-- the Hillel Academy. It's a Hebrew school. And there, my second daughter was born, Ita, named after my mother. And, and then I moved to Toronto in 1961. I moved to Toronto. I came here as the principal of the Bobov School, a Yiddish school. As a Yiddishist, I had published already in Montreal two collections of poems, Yiddish poems I begin to write after the war. And so they knew me already, a Yiddishist. I was principal for two years. When they build a new building, they brought another principal there, a member of their party, so I left the Bobov and I went to Beth Tzedec. I work there as a teacher in the evening school, in the afternoon school, and the day school. And then I became curator. I gave up the day school. I became curator of the Beth Tzedec Museum, but also librarian. And there I-- until I retired. And I'm still connected with the Beth Tzedec Museum. I go in on Sundays.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you missed your degree in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh yeah. I-- After, after I studied, I, I, I got the diploma as a teacher. I wanted to continue. I, I continued to study in the summers in New York at the Jewish Theological Seminary, the Teacher's Institute. I went there for four summers. I took the courses, and then I wrote a thesis, which was published in a book in Hebrew, a Hebrew little book on Shelomoh Maimon, an 18th-century philosopher, his attitude toward Judaism, toward the study. I got a master's degree out here. And I did also-- when I came back, I did also a BA at Toronto University. And so this, this delayed my, my book publication. I published two little books in Montreal. But when I finished studies, I published a, a big collection, a big book of collected poems, Yiddish, called for which I got the Segal Prize. I got the Segal Prize, and then the other books-- I won three awards from the book committee of the Jewish Congress of the Federation of Greater Toronto. And I published two years ago this novel, which describe the life, my, my childhood, in the form of a novel, in the third person, my childhood, my youth, all the environment and milieu, which is translated now into English, the And I'm preparing-- and I'm working on another, another book of poems, and it will be called-- translations from Yiddish, my own, other people translate it. It will be called Hopefully next year when I-- to my 75th birthday, hopefully it will appear.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel-- you came from very humble beginnings. Your father a fine man, but he didn't have much formal education. He was stitching boots, and that's all you did before the war was stitch boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you have a master's degree, you have a BA--

speaker: interviewee
text: Published books and awards.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you're such an educated man, well-spoken. How do you feel about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel, I feel, I mean, I achieved. I learned-- I'm proficient in, in Yiddish, of course, my native language, in Polish, in Russian. I read Russian. And Hebrew I learn very well. Published a book. And I'm working on my English. I think I-- I translated my book myself, but I gave it to read to people, a few people that knew English to correct it, but I translated myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I think at the beginning I was-- it was hard in Canada. Two years, I worked in the factory, and when I came, became a teacher, it was also hard. I had the problems with discipline with little children. With the grownups, I taught in the teaching center here in Toronto. When I got my degree I was a kid, but with little children, I was-- it was very hard, so I was bitter disappointed. But I appreciate Canada, a free country, and all the possibilities they gave me. With all the experiences that your family went through, do you have a message? You were a teacher. What's your message to young people about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: To young people-- the first, the first message is with my father. When I-- can I read that? I have a sonnet.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: At end. Yeah. What he told me, he said when I went away, and I-- it was a scene, a whole scene in the, in the courtyard, mother crying, the children crying, and my father gave me 10 zlotys in the pocket, and he told me, 'Remain a Jew. May God bless you on your way. Remain a Jew.' So this is one. Continue Jewish tradition. The second message, I would say, to be on the guard with anti-Semitism, to take-- because Hitler, I later read and he wrote there, plainly, clearly that in case a war will break out, this will be the end of European Jewry. You take-- we have to take these words seriously, of the anti-Semites. If the Jews would take seriously, they could-- the Polish Jewry could be saved, they could evacuate themselves into Russia. Tremendous country. They were all craftsmen. They could settle themselves. Most of them would survive and came back. The leadership, especially leadership, should be responsible, should be on guard, should stay like captains. If there is a crisis, should stay with the people, not run away, because our great rabbi, [INAUDIBLE] rabbi, the [INAUDIBLE] rabbi run away. They saved themselves and left thousands of their followers there. Political leaders run away. It, it was dangerous for them, but they shouldn't run away. They should have stayed and be prepared, have alternative plans what to do in case of crisis.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last question I want to ask about anti-Semitism-- you experienced a lot in Poland, probably in Russia also. How do you feel about it today?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia I didn't experience too much. I remember that officially it was forbidden. We had a party in Spassk, and the, and the balabusta, the Russian woman, that time we had a party was drunk and she said, [NON-ENGLISH], and [INAUDIBLE] a Jew was called [INAUDIBLE] usually, and [NON-ENGLISH] was an insult. And she was afraid later after she came to herself. She was afraid that we would tell her that she insulted us, but we didn't. I did not find much. There were, there were-- here and there, you heard that Jews run away from the front. In Poland, in the '30s, there was anti-Semitism. Our school-- I describe in my book, our school was attacked by children of another Polish school. With stones, with sticks they attacked us as we left the school. And the principal, the Polish principal, had, had to call police before they disappeared. So in Poland we experience. After the war, there was some resentment against Jews because they said Jews participate in the government, in the KGB. At the beginning they didn't have intelligent people to manage, so they used [INAUDIBLE], a lot of former communist and so on. There was some resentment. I travel to [INAUDIBLE] and there were, yeah-- there were areas of Poland where they took, took out Jews, and they shot them from the train. But we're in Silesia, where we were in a compound. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was safe. Yeah. I used to travel for the organization to speak and so on, lecture in the towns of Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you wanted to read us a sonnet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'll read a sonnet. Can I-- I will now read three poems about my family and my hometown. Now the first one, 'Otwock,' is from my book published in 1990 by Mosaic Press. 'Otwock, my Jewish hometown. My soul cries out for you, laments your bitter, undeserved end. Alive and well, full of vigor and innocence I left you to return to weeds and stones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Swiderska, street of my childhood, named after the quietly floating Swider, with its clear, with its clear beaches, fragrant pines. Why did I desert you? Why did I not share to the end your days of fright and hunger, the last road to Treblinka's gate?'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] These two poems are for my new book that I hope to publish in 1940, this year. 'Night of Vigil,' I describe the night when I left my home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'That night of vigil, of last parting, burns in my memory, an eternal flame. Last glances, handshakes and embraces, last whispering of dear names. Mother's face in grief contorted, her tears streaming from her eyes. Father, [INAUDIBLE] in muted pain, steadying for the last goodbye. 'Remain a Jew. Remember. May God protect you on your way.' His words end like a solemn testament as I run reluctantly away. From now on, anguish and regret will scald my soul until my death.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other one is 'Looking at a Family Portrait,' which was done in 1938, before my sister left for Israel, Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Looking at a Family Portrait.' 'My pure and innocent six doves. They hunted all of you. They drove into ghettos, into camps of death. Only I remain to ponder and regret. Your faces shine from the darkened frame. I mutter silently each, each one of your names and remember our nest of long ago. Before it was wiped out by the deadly foe, before the senseless, merciless decree came down on all of you and fated me to carry always, like the holy tablets, too, the hallowed memory of all of you. Till the last of days, with reverence and love, my pure and innocent six doves.'

speaker: interviewer
text: They're very lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my dear wife, Frieda, from the home [NON-ENGLISH]. We lived in Otwock on the same street, and we attended for five years the same school, the Polish public school. We started to go out steadily the year before, the year before the war, 1938, '39. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we were separated. She was sent to Siberia with her sister, her younger sister, but we met again in 1942, I think. Yeah, 1942. And we got married. And we have-- we came back to Poland and to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two married daughters and one grandson. He is one year and eight months, yes? Michael. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we hope to have enjoyment from this grandson, and we hope to continue to live in Canada, land of freedom and peace. And we hope that these things that we experience will never again be happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a, a family picture that was taken in 1938 by the end of the summer, when my older sister left for Palestine. She was a, a Hatzair. She was prepared on a Zionist community in Poland for seven years, and then she left illegally for Palestine. And this is the picture. She's standing there in the middle in the upper row. To her left, I, I am there, and to the left of me is my sister Tama, younger sister Tama. And to the right of my older sister is my youngest sister, [NON-ENGLISH], Rachel. On the second to row from the, from the right is my little brother, Goedl, my father, Moishe, and my mother, Ita, and my youngest little sister, Malka, or Malkala. My older sister, Sheindl, left for Palestine, and she survived. She stayed there. She had a family. She had three sons and grandchildren. I left in the first week of the war. All the others perished. And they were sent to Treblinka in August 1940. This picture was taken in the summer of 1938 or '39. And this was a, a group from the youth organization [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a newly found Bundist youth organization in Otwock. And we took it on an excursion. We walked to the Medem Sanatorium. This was a children's sanatorium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] famous in Poland, in Miedzeszyn. It was about eight, 10 kilometers from Otwock. And we went there by foot in the summer to visit. Of these, I am, I am there in the upper row, the second from right, and my wife is in the bottom row in the center in the white spring coat. And only one that I know of this group except us two survived. I met him. He's in the center in the upper row, to the left of me. I met him. And the others, all of them, perished in Otwock, in the ghetto. This picture is dated. It's August the 19th, 1942, the day when 8,000 Otwock Jews were sent, dispatched to Treblinka. We see here the railway tracks, and people, the Jews, gathered there, awaiting for the arrival of the train. It may be taken by Germans or by a Pole. It's included in the memorial book of Otwock and Karczew, which was published in the 1960s in Israel. This is the memorial book of Otwock and Karczew, the opening page, the front page. It tells memorial book to memorialize the memory of the destroyed Jewish congregation of Otwock and Karczew. The editor is Shimon Kanc. It was published in Tel Aviv in 1968 by the organization, the Landsmanshaft of Otwock in Israel. It-- yeah. It, it includes at the end memoirs of life before the war and memoirs of survivors. And at the end, it has a list of the Jews that perished and died, a list of a few thousand martyrs. They're listed alphabetically by name. My family is included there. This is a picture that was taken in 1940 in the town of Spassk in central Russia, where we arrived. We registered, a few thousand people registered for work in Russia. And these six were picked up by a representative of the leather factory of Spassk. And the first day we arrived, we were given new clothes, and these are the shirts we were given, and the leather boots. And we were settled, each one for work. I'm standing in the top row, at the far left. To the right of me is a friend of mine, Joseph Weingarten, with whom I studied at the evening school in Spassk. This is a photograph of my dear wife, Frieda, taken in 1939 in the winter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was in Otwock. We were friends going out at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 17 years old then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her full-- and her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. She is called Frieda. This is a family picture of, of my wife's, my wife's family, taken in 1938

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: In summer. In the, in the center is her mother, [NON-ENGLISH]. And to her, to her right is her daughter, my wife's sister, and a cousin. To her left, another sister and her husband, which came later to Lodz. And at her far left is Frieda, and standing behind is her sister [NON-ENGLISH] who survived together with Frieda in Siberia. And she lives here in Toronto. She has two daughters and four grandchildren. All the others perished. This is a picture of the Cunard White Star steamship by which we arrived to Canada from Le Havre to Quebec in the summer, in the end of June 1949. This is a picture of myself in the Beth Tzedec Museum where I was curator for 25 years or more. And I am holding a rare Italian Torah crown, a silver Italian Torah crown. This is an award. It reads, 'The book committee of the Jewish Federation of greater Toronto takes pride in presenting the 1992 Izzy and Betty Foundation Book Award to Sam Simchovitch for creative writing in poetry.' It's signed by Adam-- Professor Adam Fuerstenberg, chairman of the book committee, June 1992. And that award was given for my collection of English poetry, And it is interesting to know that Izzy Kirshenbaum is a survivor from my city, a [INAUDIBLE] from Otwock, survived Otwock. He lives in Toronto as a businessman. And he provided the funds for that award. And this is of our family, a picture. Here I am with my wife. My-- in the middle is my older daughter, Miriam, and there is our second daughter, the younger daughter, Ita. It was taken in Ottawa in the late 1950s. This is our grandson, Michael. He is one year and eight months old. He is the son of our daughter Ita and her husband, [INAUDIBLE].
